html:
Civeo Corp. (44C1.F / CVEO)

Civeo is not the fantasy version of the prior draft. It is not net-cash, not trading at 3x EBITDA, and not already printing $100 million of equity free cash flow. The real thesis is more nuanced and still attractive: this is a tiny, ignored workforce-accommodations owner that the market still labels as oil-sands services, while its value is shifting toward Australian met-coal lodging, asset-light facilities management, and a call option on North American LNG, power, and data-center construction.

At the current 44C1.F price of about €28.60, with NYSE CVEO around $34, Civeo’s equity value is roughly $365 million to $380 million and enterprise value is roughly $565 million to $580 million using March 31 net debt of $198.9 million. Against management’s 2026 adjusted EBITDA guide of $85 million to $90 million, the stock is not optically screaming cheap at 6.4x to 6.8x EV/EBITDA. Against the last twelve months adjusted EBITDA of $98.1 million, it is closer to 5.9x. The upside comes from what that 2026 guide excludes or understates: full integration of Qantac, recovering Canadian margins, further buybacks, and a 2027-2028 infrastructure cycle that could turn underused North American lodging assets back into earning assets.

The May 1 quarter was the important reset. Revenue grew 20% to $172.7 million and adjusted EBITDA rose 78% to $22.5 million, per Civeo’s Q1 2026 release. Australia generated $123.0 million of revenue and $21.8 million of adjusted EBITDA. Canada, which had looked like the broken segment, moved from negative $0.8 million of adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2025 to positive $5.2 million in Q1 2026. The stock is being valued as if Canada is a melting ice cube and Australia is a cyclical coal appendage. The quarter showed something better: Australia is the cash-flow base, Canada has operating leverage after cost cuts, and the mobile camp fleet may now have external demand from infrastructure customers.

Australia is the anchor. In 2025, Australia was 72% of revenue, generated record segment revenue of $460 million, and produced $89 million of adjusted EBITDA before corporate costs. Civeo owns 10,318 Australian rooms across twelve villages, including 8,856 rooms in the Bowen Basin, where it says it is the largest workforce-hospitality provider. The May 2025 Qantac acquisition added 1,368 Bowen Basin rooms and cost about US$68 million in cash, or roughly $50,000 per room before considering contracts and land. Civeo’s whole enterprise value today is only around $22,000 per owned room across the 26,500-room owned network, before giving any value to the 19,500 customer-owned rooms it manages. That replacement-cost lens matters because new remote accommodations are slow to permit, expensive to build, and hard to staff.

The commodity backdrop is better than the stock label suggests. Civeo is not a coal producer, but Bowen Basin occupancy depends on customer economics. Australian premium hard coking coal has recently been around $235 to $240 per tonne, above the level management framed as the modernized customer comfort zone after inflation. That does not guarantee new occupancy, because miners are still cost-disciplined and diesel/labor inflation is real, but it undercuts the bear case that Australia is heading into an immediate demand cliff.

The Canadian segment is the torque. Q1 Canadian billed rooms rose 17% year over year, gross margin improved sharply, and management said the strength was not mainly a pull-forward. Oil near $100 WTI is supportive for oil-sands production activity, but the more interesting angle is not oil. Civeo has about 2,500 mobile camp rooms ready in Western Canada and says about 7,000 oil-sands lodge rooms could be redeployed for the right infrastructure project. Management described a North American bid pipeline above $1.5 billion and tied the opportunity set to LNG, power, and data centers, with meaningful contribution more likely in 2027 and beyond. That is the wrong-label setup: a forgotten oilfield-adjacent lodging stock may have scarce, immediately deployable beds for AI-power and infrastructure construction.

Capital allocation is also unusually aggressive for a microcap value stock. Civeo repurchased 2.3 million shares for about $54 million in 2025, equal to roughly 17% of the year-end 2024 share count, and bought another 0.5 million shares for $14.4 million in Q1 2026. Since the program began in 2021, the company says it has retired 37% of its common shares. The board also authorized another 10% buyback after the existing authorization. This is not idle cash hoarding. The risk is that Q1 buybacks increased net debt to 2.2x bank-adjusted EBITDA, but the April 2026 credit amendment pushed maturity to 2030 and expanded capacity to $285 million, so the balance sheet has runway.

The market setup is still obscure. Retail chatter is almost nonexistent, Stocktwits sentiment is effectively unusable because message volume is so low, and Reddit references are mostly automated filing posts with minimal engagement. Sell-side coverage is thin, with Stifel’s latest target at $37. This is exactly the kind of stock that can sit mispriced until one or two quarters make the new label obvious.

The risk is that 2026 is a transition year, not a harvest year. Management’s guide implies GAAP losses, adjusted EBITDA below the LTM run rate, and only moderate free cash flow after capex, cash taxes, and interest. Australia faces labor shortages and fuel inflation. Canada remains tied to oil-sands and project timing. Fortescue and Suncor each represented more than 10% of 2025 revenue, so customer concentration is real. If management keeps buying stock while leverage rises and EBITDA disappoints, the buyback becomes a balance-sheet risk rather than an accretive capital return.

Still, the upside case is credible. A base 2027 model of $105 million to $110 million of adjusted EBITDA, $25 million to $30 million of capex, $13 million to $16 million of interest, and normalized cash taxes can produce roughly $45 million to $55 million of free cash flow. On the current market cap, that is a low-double-digit FCF yield before additional buyback shrink. If infrastructure awards move EBITDA toward $125 million to $130 million, the stock is much too cheap.

Targets from today’s €28.60 price

Bear: €18, down 37%. EBITDA falls toward $80 million, met-coal customers pull back, Canada fades again, leverage stays above 2x, and the market values the business at about 5.5x depressed EBITDA.

Base: €39, up 36%. EBITDA reaches roughly $108 million in 2027, Civeo retires another high-single-digit percentage of shares, net debt stays controlled, and the stock earns a 6x EBITDA multiple.

Bull: €62, up 117%. Australia holds, Canada wins LNG/power/data-center work, EBITDA approaches $130 million, the market values the company as scarce infrastructure lodging capacity rather than oilfield services, and buybacks amplify per-share value.
